topic: Traits - Index and IndexMut Operator Overloading

turns.content: I'm trying to implement the Index trait for my Vec2 struct so I can use square brackets like other arrays, but my implementation is always returning an empty array instead of a tuple index.

It sounds like you might be forgetting that the Index trait requires a generic type parameter Idx. You need to return a concrete tuple of indices from your method, not just a placeholder. The standard signature for this is fn __index__(self: &Self) -> Result<Idx, Self::Error>.

I think I have that right in my code, but when I try to access an element at position (0, 1), it seems to panic with a generic error message rather than returning the actual value. Is there something wrong with how I'm calling the trait methods?

The issue is likely that you are implementing Index but not IndexMut, and your implementation might be assuming the index type is always i32 when it should actually be using the generic Idx parameter. If you want mutable access, you must also implement IndexMut which returns an Option<&mut Self::Target>.

Ah okay, so I need to handle both traits separately? But when I try to implement the trait for my struct, the compiler says I'm missing a generic type definition somewhere in the implementation block itself?

Yes, that is exactly what's happening. You need to define your Index and IndexMut implementations with explicit generics like impl<T: SomeTrait> MyStruct for T. If you are trying to implement it without the generic parameters, the compiler won't know what Idx type to use.

I managed to get the basic syntax working now, but when I try to use multi-dimensional indexing like (0, 1) on a tuple index, it still doesn't work as expected. The documentation says I should return a tuple of indices, but my code is just returning a single index.

You need to ensure your Index trait implementation returns the correct tuple type for your specific dimensionality. For a two-dimensional vector, you must return (usize, usize) as the Idx type. If you are returning a single index, that won't match the expected tuple structure.

I updated my code to return the tuple indices now, but I'm still getting an error saying I can't call square brackets on this type. It seems like there's a mismatch between what I implemented and how the trait is being used.

The problem might be that you haven't properly bound your Index implementation to the correct generic parameters in your struct definition. You need to make sure the trait bounds are correctly applied so that when someone writes vec2[0][1], the compiler knows exactly what type of index to expect.
I think I finally got it working, but now I'm wondering if there's a better way to handle panics versus checked access. Should I always panic when an invalid index is provided?

While panicking is common in low-level Rust code for performance reasons, the Index trait specifically expects you to return either a Result or Option depending on your design choice. If you want checked access without panics, you should implement Index to return an Err variant when invalid indices are provided.

topic: Traits - Index and IndexMut Operator Overloading

turns.content: Hey, I'm back from my five-year break. I saw some code using [] on a struct and it looked cool. How do you make that work in Rust?
That's the Index trait! You implement it so your type supports bracket notation. For mutable access, you need IndexMut too.
Got it. But I thought [] just worked on everything? Why do I have to write this trait block?
Rust is strict about operator overloading; only types that explicitly implement Index or IndexMut get the [] syntax.
Okay, so I just need to add these two traits? Does it support all kinds of brackets?
Index covers read access and IndexMut covers writes. There's also Deref for dereferencing, but for [] you really care about Index and IndexMut.
What if I want to check bounds before indexing? Does Rust automatically do that?
No, it panics by default. You can choose to panic or return an Option via checked_index for safer access.
Panic sounds scary. Is there a way to make it fail gracefully?
Yes, implement IndexMut::checked_index which returns an Option instead of panicking on bad indices.
That's great. And what about 2D arrays or tuples? Can I do [][0]?
You can implement Index for a tuple type with multiple indices, like (i32, i32) to support nested bracket access.
Wait, so I don't need separate traits for 2D? Just one trait?
Exactly. One Index implementation with a tuple index type handles multi-dimensional indexing cleanly.
I think I get it now. So the key is choosing the right trait and managing panics manually?
Yes, that's the core of operator overloading for Index-like traits in Rust.

topic: Traits - Index and IndexMut Operator Overloading

turns.content: Hey, I've been staring at the docs for traits::index and index_mut but I still can't wrap my head around why we need those two separate traits instead of just having one. It feels redundant to me.

That's a totally normal confusion when you're coming back from a five-year break! Think of it like a regular expression and a regex replace function in your old language; one reads the pattern, the other actually changes things. In Rust, index is for reading values without touching memory, while index_mut allows you to safely modify data inside the collection.
Okay so if I want to add a new element at position zero in my vector, which trait do I implement?

You need index_mut because you're changing the internal state. If you only implemented index, your code would panic or behave unexpectedly since there's no way to tell the compiler you intend to write.
Got it. But I'm worried about panics crashing my whole program if I mess up the indices. Is there a safer alternative?
Exactly! That's why checked methods exist in std collections like checked_index or get_mut. They return an Option so you can handle errors gracefully instead of panicking.
Can I use these traits for multi-dimensional arrays too, like matrices?
Yes! You can implement index with a tuple index type that handles pairs or triples of indices. This lets you access elements using bracket notation just like in regular languages.
So if I have a 2D array, I'd need to define my own index trait implementation?
Right. You create an index type that holds two i32 values and implement the trait for it on your matrix struct.
Wait, does this work with borrowed slices or just owned data?
You can implement both. For slices you use the slice-specific traits, but for custom arrays you implement them directly on your type.
Ah! So I just need to make sure my index type matches what the trait expects and then I can use [] everywhere. That clicks!
Exactly! Once you get that mental model of how traits::index works with your custom types, it becomes pretty straightforward.

topic: Traits - Index and IndexMut Operator Overloading

turns.content: I'm trying to port a Node.js array handling library to Rust, and I keep hitting this wall where my code uses bracket notation everywhere. In JavaScript it's just magic under the hood, but in Rust I need to actually implement something specific for that square-bracket syntax.

That square-bracket syntax is handled by implementing the Index trait on your type so the [] operator becomes a method call internally. You can also do the same thing with IndexMut if you need to support assignment, and these are defined in the std::ops module.
So I just write impl Index for MyType? And then how does that actually let me use [] without writing a custom accessor function?

Yes, once you implement the trait the compiler automatically rewrites your [] calls into calling the get method on the index type. You don't write any special syntax for it; the operator just becomes a normal method call to that trait implementation.
That sounds great, but what if I need to mutate values inside? Does IndexMut do exactly the same thing as Index?

IndexMut is a separate trait that lets you use [] = assignment instead of requiring a .set() method. It's very similar to Index but provides the mutable version so your Rust code behaves more like JavaScript arrays.
I'm worried about safety though, because panicking on bad indices feels wrong for production code. Is there a way to avoid that panic?
You can implement std::ops::Index instead of the simpler trait and return an Option or Result from your get method, which lets you handle out-of-bounds indices gracefully without crashing.
What about multi-dimensional structures like matrices? I need to support indexing with tuples, not just single values. How do I implement that?
For multi-dimensional access you can implement Index for your type using a tuple index pattern where the trait is implemented specifically for types like (usize, usize) pairs.

Oh wow, so I don't need to write custom getters for every dimension. Just one implementation that handles tuples? That feels like a real aha moment on how clean Rust can be compared to the verbose array handling in other languages.

Exactly, and if you combine Index with IndexMut you get both read and write support without extra boilerplate. It really shows how traits let you express complex array behaviors with minimal code compared to other languages.

topic: Traits - Index and IndexMut Operator Overloading

turns.content: I'm struggling with implementing the Index trait for a two-dimensional grid in my Rust code. I thought if I just implement Index, it would automatically support both read and write operations like C++ iterators do.

That's a common misconception. In Rust, implementing the Index trait only gives you read access via [] syntax; you need to separately implement the IndexMut trait for mutable writes. Think of it like a library card: having an index (Index) lets you look up information, but you still need a membership pass or a specific key (IndexMut) to actually modify or write data into that location.

Okay, so I understand Index is for reading and IndexMut is for writing. But what happens if someone tries to use [] on my struct without implementing both traits? Will it just return an error or panic?

If you try to call the [] operator without implementing either trait, the compiler will simply refuse to compile your code with a clear error message. It won't silently panic at runtime because Rust is designed to be safe and catch mistakes before execution starts.
That's good to know. But what if I want to check bounds first instead of panicking? Can I do that with the Index trait?

The Index and IndexMut traits don't provide built-in methods for bounds checking; they assume the indices you pass are valid. To implement safe access, you'd need to write your own logic using standard library types like usize or Option<YourType> to handle cases where indices go out of range before calling the trait methods.
I see. So for a 2D grid, I need to manually check if row and column are within bounds before accessing elements?

Exactly. You would typically implement your own helper methods or use standard library collections like Vec or HashMap internally that provide safe iteration and access patterns, then wrap them in your Index trait implementation.
What about multi-dimensional indexing? If I have a 3D array, how does the tuple index work with these traits?

For multi-dimensional arrays, you'll need to implement Index for tuples like (usize, usize, usize) alongside your basic Index implementation. The compiler will generate separate trait implementations for each tuple type so that [] works correctly regardless of dimensionality.
That makes sense. So I need to manually create Index impls for all the different tuple combinations in my 3D array?

Yes, you'll need to implement Index and IndexMut for each specific tuple type your application uses. Rust allows this flexibility so you can tailor how indexing works without needing a generic solution that doesn't fit your data structure.
I'm still confused about how the compiler knows which trait to use when I write my code. Is it automatic?

The Rust compiler performs type inference and will automatically select the appropriate trait implementation based on the context of your code, so you don't have to manually specify which trait you're using in most cases.
Great. One last thing: what if I want to avoid implementing Index and IndexMut separately? Is there a simpler way?
There isn't really a single trait that covers both reading and writing; you'll need to keep both Index and IndexMut separate unless you're using a different language or abstraction layer.
